focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

The bus from Miami dropped her in Gainesville before the heat got rude. The sidewalks still held the night's damp in their cracks, but the sky had already gone that hard white-blue Florida liked to put up before noon, a color with no mercy in it. Tiana crossed campus with her document folder tucked under her arm and the strap of her bag burning a line into her shoulder. Families moved around her in small, practiced clusters, carrying the kind of ordinary equipment that had started to look luxurious to her: refillable water bottles, envelopes clipped together, printed campus maps, a parent who knew where to stand next.

Nobody was cartoon rich. That made it worse.

One girl walked with a father in a pest-control polo and a mother still wearing hospital clogs, both of them arguing softly over whether the student union was the brick building or the glass one. A Haitian grandmother in a white skirt kept slapping her grandson's wrist away from his phone and telling him to look up at the campus God had provided. A white boy with acne scars and a Publix tan pushed a rolling suitcase while his mother read from a stapled checklist with the serious face people usually saved for medicine. They all looked tired. They all looked as if taking a day or two off work had cost them something. They had still come together. They still had a somebody to say you go get your ID while I hold the place in line.

For one stupid minute she hated all of them for having infrastructure.

Then she hated herself for that too, because she knew exactly what her mother had done to get her here at all. Marcia had ironed her blouse on the kitchen counter with the little travel iron that hissed and spat rust at the edges, then folded two ten-dollar bills into the back sleeve of the folder and t...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...sheets curled at the edges.

The clippers were under a faded barbershop cape Delroy had picked up years ago from some cousin who had started cutting hair in a garage and then vanished to Broward. Silver body, black guard snapped on crooked, cord wrapped with electrical tape near the base. Heavy enough to mean something in the hand.

Micah had used them on Dre once, just to clean the neck. Then on two little boys down the block before church. Then on himself, badly. Marcia had called the back of his head a crime scene and fixed it with kitchen scissors while cussing under her breath. But people paid for a clean line if the neighborhood barbershop was booked and payday hadn't hit right.

He slid the clippers into the front pocket of his backpack.

From the kitchenette, Delroy started saying something about not making a bad week worse. Marcia cut him off. Oil hissed. A spoon hit the sink. Tiana's chair scraped back. The whole apartment kept happening three feet from every other part of itself.

Micah pulled his phone from the charger.

He stared at Dre's number for a moment, thumb hovering.

Then he typed: `What time again`

The reply came almost before he had locked the screen.

`4:45. Don't dress scary.`

Micah laughed under his breath. It was the first real laugh of the day. It felt wrong in his mouth and good anyway.

He shoved the phone into his pocket, zipped the bag, and pushed the suspension packet under the bed with the side of his foot until it disappeared into the dust and lost pens.

The clippers made a solid shape in the backpack now, more convincing than the stack of assignments, more useful than any explanation waiting on the other side of the wall.

When Marcia called that dinner was ready, he said, "Coming," and meant it only in the smallest possible way.
